cities.name: Algonquin
cities.intro: Algonquin is a village on the Fox River straddling the McHenry and Kane County line — approximately 78 percent of the village area is in McHenry County, with the remainder in Kane. The Fox River runs north-south through the heart of the downtown. Algonquin is bordered to the north by Lake in the Hills, and Cary lies to the north along the Fox River corridor.

cities.name: Antioch
cities.intro:
Antioch is a village in Lake County, Illinois, located approximately 60 miles north of Chicago and 50 miles south of Milwaukee. The village borders Wisconsin and sits within the Chain O'Lakes waterway system. Settlers arrived in the 1830s, and the name 'Antioch' was given in 1843; the region was initially called 'Sequoit,' meaning 'winding,' after a local creek.

The village encompasses 8.63 square miles and features multiple glacial lakes including Antioch Lake, Lake Marie, and Redwing Slough Lake. The village lies in a gently rolling moraine landscape dominated by lakes of glacial origin.

cities.name: Arlington Heights
cities.intro:
Arlington Heights is a village in Cook County, Illinois, located approximately 25 miles northwest of Chicago. The village spans 16.64 square miles across portions of Wheeling, Elk Grove, and Palatine townships. As of the 2020 census, Arlington Heights had a population of 77,676, making it the 15th-most populous municipality in Illinois.

The area transitioned from Potawatomi land to European settlement following the 1833 Treaty of Chicago. The community was originally called West Wheeling, then Dunton, before being officially renamed Arlington Heights in 1874. Arlington Park Racetrack, founded in 1927, hosted prestigious events including the Arlington Million; in 2021, the Chicago Bears signed a purchase agreement for the property to develop a new stadium.

cities.name: Barrington
cities.intro:
Barrington is a village in Cook and Lake counties, Illinois, incorporated on February 16, 1865. The village name derives from Great Barrington, Massachusetts, in reference to the New England origins of many early settlers. The Historic Preservation Overlay District, established in 2001, encompasses examples of Victorian, Victorian Gothic, Queen Anne, and other late-19th-century architectural forms.

Notable Barrington structures include the Octagon House (also known as Hawley House), listed on the National Register of Historic Places, and the Catlow Theater, which opened in May 1927 and operates today as a single-screen theater. Cuba Marsh Forest Preserve — a 782-acre wetland with three miles of crushed-gravel trail — lies northeast of the village core.

cities.name: Barrington Hills
cities.intro:
Barrington Hills is a village located approximately 40 miles northwest of Chicago, incorporated in 1957. The village spans approximately 29 square miles across four counties — Cook, Kane, McHenry, and Lake. A 5-acre minimum zoning restriction has been in effect on new construction since 1963, preserving the area's equestrian heritage and estate properties.

Beginning around 1900, Chicago business executives purchased farmland and created large estates in the area. The Barrington Hills Country Club, with its eighteen-hole golf course, was established in 1921 on 200 acres of land donated by early founders. The village includes significant historical structures such as the Barrington Center Church (built 1853) and St. John Nepomucene Chapel (completed 1873).

cities.name: Bartlett
cities.intro:
Bartlett is a village spanning three counties — Cook, DuPage, and Kane — in Illinois. The village was named after Luther Bartlett, who in 1873 contributed funds and half of his 40-acre woodlot toward the construction of a train depot. Bartlett was incorporated in 1892 and experienced the majority of its population growth in the 1980s.

The BAPS Shri Swaminarayan Mandir, a Hindu temple that opened in Bartlett in 2004, is the largest traditional Hindu mandir constructed in the United States, spanning 30 acres with materials including Turkish limestone and Italian marble. Villa Olivia, a golf and winter sports resort, also operates in the village.

cities.name: Buffalo Grove
cities.intro:
Buffalo Grove is a village in Lake and Cook counties, Illinois, located approximately 30 miles northwest of downtown Chicago. The area was originally inhabited by the Potawatomi, and the village name derives from the English translation of the Potawatomi name for Buffalo Creek. Catholic German immigrants established St. Mary's Church and School in the 1850s.

Buffalo Grove incorporated in 1958 with 164 residents and grew rapidly in the following decades — the 1970s produced an 80 percent population increase, the third-highest rate in the northwest suburbs. The Buffalo Grove Metra station, opened in 1996, serves commuters on the North Central Service line to Chicago's Union Station.

cities.name: Cary
cities.intro: Cary is a village in McHenry County, Illinois, located on the Fox River within Algonquin Township. The village is bordered by Crystal Lake to the north and Algonquin to the south.

cities.name: Crystal Lake
cities.intro:
Crystal Lake is a city in McHenry County, Illinois, named for the lake of the same name located approximately 1.6 miles southwest of the city's downtown. The Crystal Lake Park District manages Main Beach along the lake. The neighboring Village of Lakewood occupies approximately 30 percent of the lake's shoreline.

Veteran Acres Park, a 140-acre recreational facility maintained by the Crystal Lake Park District, is among the city's principal parks. Route 14 forms a major commercial corridor through the area between Route 31 and Dole Avenue.

cities.name: Deer Park
cities.intro: Deer Park is a village spanning Lake and Cook counties, Illinois, incorporated in 1957. The village maintains a green belt bordered by two large natural areas, with residential zoning emphasizing single-family homes on lots of one acre or larger. Deer Park Town Center and Town Center Promenade are notable shopping and dining destinations within the village. The Vehe Farm, designated as an Illinois Centennial Farm, represents the area's agricultural heritage.

cities.name: Deerfield
cities.intro:
Deerfield is a village in Lake and Cook counties, Illinois, located approximately 28 miles north of downtown Chicago on the North Shore. The village borders Bannockburn, Riverwoods, Highland Park, and Northbrook. Deerfield was originally settled in 1835 by Horace Lamb and Jacob B. Cadwell as Cadwell's Corner, and was renamed around 1850 by settler John Millen in honor of his hometown of Deerfield, Massachusetts. The village was formally incorporated in 1903.

The Deerfield Historic Village, maintained by the Deerfield Area Historical Society, includes five historic buildings, among them the 1837 Caspar Ott Cabin. Deerfield is the corporate headquarters of Walgreens Boots Alliance, Baxter Healthcare, and Fortune Brands Home & Security.

cities.name: Evanston
cities.intro: Evanston is a city in Cook County, Illinois, located along Lake Michigan immediately south of Wilmette and bordered by Chicago to the south. Northwestern University's lakefront campus occupies a wooded bluff along the lakeshore. By the 1920s, Evanston had earned the sobriquet 'The City of Homes' in reference to its concentration of mansions and the work of numerous prominent architects, particularly along the lakefront.

cities.name: Fox Lake
cities.intro:
Fox Lake is a village spanning Lake and McHenry counties, Illinois, located approximately 50 miles north of Chicago. The village was incorporated on December 15, 1906, and certified by the state on April 13, 1907.

The community sits among the Chain O'Lakes system, with the village center positioned on the east shore of Pistakee Lake and the south shores of Nippersink Lake and Fox Lake. These connected waterways form part of a recreational water system that drains southwest toward the Illinois River. At its early-20th-century peak as a summer resort, the area hosted 50 hotels and 2,000 cottages.

cities.name: Glencoe
cities.intro:
Glencoe is a lakefront village in northeastern Cook County, Illinois, situated along Lake Michigan. The Chicago Botanic Garden is located at the village's northwest corner, technically just outside village limits.

Glencoe is home to the Ravine Bluffs subdivision, which contains seven Frank Lloyd Wright-designed houses, the Wright-designed Sylvan Road Bridge (1915), and three sculptural markers; two additional individually-built Wright homes stand nearby. The subdivision represents one of the most concentrated collections of Wright residential architecture in the region.

cities.name: Glenview
cities.intro: Glenview is a village in Cook County, Illinois, with Wheeling to the northwest, Northbrook to the northeast, and Northfield to the north. The village's largest planned community, The Glen, was developed on the 1,121-acre site of the former Glenview Naval Air Station after the base's closure in 1995.

cities.name: Gurnee
cities.intro:
Gurnee is a village in Lake County, Illinois, founded in 1928 and named after former Chicago mayor Walter S. Gurnee. The village spans 13.58 square miles, situated along the Des Plaines River and bisected by Interstate 94.

Gurnee is the location of Six Flags Great America (opened 1976), Hurricane Harbor Chicago water park, Gurnee Mills outlet mall, and Great Wolf Lodge resort — venues that together attract approximately 23 million visitors annually. Six Flags Great America and Hurricane Harbor Chicago together employ approximately 3,315 people.

cities.name: Hawthorn Woods
cities.intro:
Hawthorn Woods is a village in Lake County, Illinois, spanning portions of Fremont and Ela townships and located approximately 40 miles northwest of downtown Chicago. The village was officially incorporated in 1958. The Potawatomi tribe inhabited the area before American settlement; later development was shaped by German and Dutch immigrants.

Major roadways through the village include Midlothian Road, Old McHenry Road, Algonquin Road, and Half Day Road. The Elgin, Joliet and Eastern Railway also runs through the village's footprint.

cities.name: Highland Park
cities.intro:
Highland Park is a city in Lake County, Illinois, situated along Lake Michigan with Highwood to the north and Glencoe to the south. The city's geography is shaped by deep wooded ravines that extend up to a mile inland from the lakefront.

The Ravinia District, originally established as an artists' colony, is the location of the Ravinia Festival — an open-air pavilion seating approximately 3,200 people that has served as the summer home of the Chicago Symphony Orchestra since 1936. St. John's Avenue and Port Clinton Square in Highland Park are named after settlements that preceded the city's formal establishment.

cities.name: Highwood
cities.intro:
Highwood is a city in Moraine Township, Lake County, Illinois, situated on a ridge approximately 100 feet above the elevation of Lake Michigan, between Highland Park to the south and Fort Sheridan and Lake Forest to the north.

In the 1920s, a substantial number of Northern Italian immigrants — many originally from Modena — settled in Highwood, establishing the Italian-American community that has shaped the city's identity. Highwood today is recognized as a destination for North Shore dining and nightlife.

cities.name: Hoffman Estates
cities.intro:
Hoffman Estates is a village primarily in Cook County, Illinois, with a small portion extending into Kane County. The area was developed starting in 1954 when Sam and Jack Hoffman, owners of a father-son construction company, purchased 160 acres of land. The village was incorporated on September 23, 1959.

The Now Arena (formerly Sears Centre Arena) is home to the Windy City Bulls of the NBA G League. Hoffman Estates served as the headquarters of Sears, Roebuck and Company, which relocated to the Prairie Stone Business Park in 1993. Major employers in the village include St. Alexius Medical Center and Siemens Medical Systems.

cities.name: Inverness
cities.intro:
Inverness is a village in Cook County, Illinois. The area was initially settled in 1836 as Deer Grove. The modern community emerged in 1926 when developer Arthur T. McIntosh acquired farmland and named it Inverness, after the McIntosh clan's ancestral home in Scotland. The village was officially incorporated in 1962.

Inverness maintains a one-acre minimum lot size. The village's borders are defined by Barrington Road to the west, Roselle Road to the east, Algonquin Road to the south, and Dundee Road to the north. The Four Silos, formerly a local landmark, now houses the Village Hall (relocated in 1985).

cities.name: Kenilworth
cities.intro:
Kenilworth is a village in Cook County, Illinois, located along Lake Michigan between Winnetka to the north and Wilmette to the south. The village was founded in 1889 when Joseph Sears purchased 223.6 acres of land for a planned community, and reached the 300-resident threshold for incorporation on February 4, 1896.

The village's architectural inheritance includes Frank Lloyd Wright's Hiram Baldwin House, a Prairie School design completed in 1905. Architect George Washington Maher — a historically significant figure in Chicago-area architecture — was a Kenilworth resident.

cities.name: Kildeer
cities.intro:
Kildeer is a village in southwestern Lake County, Illinois. The village was incorporated on March 22, 1958, as a community-led response to a proposed large-scale residential development by Joseph M. Brickman. The 1958 incorporation vote established the village with 153 residents.

The village's motto — 'A unique village in a natural setting' — reflects the rural character it has maintained despite suburban development in surrounding communities. Illinois Route 53 provides highway access through the area.

cities.name: Lake Barrington
cities.intro: Lake Barrington is a village in Cuba and Wauconda townships, Lake County, Illinois, located on the shores of Lake Barrington (formerly known as Indian Lake). The village spans approximately 6.07 square miles. Adjacent communities include Port Barrington, Wauconda, Tower Lakes, North Barrington, Barrington Hills, and Fox River Grove. The village experienced its principal growth period between 1970 and 1980, when the population expanded from 347 to 2,320 residents.

cities.name: Lake Bluff
cities.intro:
Lake Bluff is a village in Lake County, Illinois, situated along Lake Michigan with Lake Forest to the south, Libertyville to the west, and Naval Station Great Lakes to the north. The village is bordered by Lake Michigan on the east and characterized by ravines and a prominent bluff overlooking the lake.

In the late 19th and early 20th centuries Lake Bluff was a destination for summer visitors drawn to the village's beach and ravines. Ravine Park, named for the geographic feature it preserves, is one of the village's principal parks.

cities.name: Lake Forest
cities.intro:
Lake Forest is a city in Lake County, Illinois, situated along Lake Michigan between Lake Bluff to the north and Highwood to the south. The city's geography is shaped by deep wooded ravines that extend up to a mile inland from the lakefront. Lake Forest is home to several historic country clubs, including the Onwentsia Club, the Knollwood Club, Conway Farms Golf Club, the Lake Forest Winter Club, and the Lake Forest Club.

Market Square, completed in 1916, anchors the city's downtown commercial district and is recognized as one of the first planned shopping centers in the United States. Lake Forest's residential architecture includes work by Howard Van Doren Shaw, David Adler, and other prominent architects of the early 20th century.

cities.name: Lake in the Hills
cities.intro: Lake in the Hills is a village in McHenry County, Illinois, incorporated in 1923. Woods Creek Lake is the principal lake within the village. Between the 1980s and 1990s, the village's population grew nearly 400 percent, with subdivisions constructed throughout the 1990s and 2000s. The village is bordered to the south by Algonquin and lies southwest of Crystal Lake.

cities.name: Lake Villa
cities.intro:
Lake Villa is a village in Lake County, Illinois, located approximately 50 miles north of Chicago. The village motto, 'Gateway to the Lake Region,' reflects its historic importance to tourism and the area's former ice industry.

The area features a gently rolling moraine landscape dominated by lakes of glacial origin, including Cedar Lake and Deep Lake. Lake Villa has a station on Metra's North Central Service, which provides daily rail service connecting Antioch to Chicago's Union Station.

cities.name: Lake Zurich
cities.intro:
Lake Zurich is a village in Lake County, Illinois, incorporated on September 29, 1896. The lake within the village was originally called Cedar Lake before being renamed Lake Zurich by early settler Seth Paine. The lake is entirely contained within village boundaries.

Housing development in the village began in the 1950s, with population growth continuing through the latter part of the 20th century. Lake Zurich is bordered by Hawthorn Woods, Deer Park, and other adjacent communities.

cities.name: Lakewood
cities.intro:
Lakewood is a village in McHenry County, Illinois, incorporated on July 10, 1933. The municipality covers approximately 4.89 square miles and shares boundaries with Crystal Lake, Lake in the Hills, Huntley, and Woodstock. The village contains six lakes, including a portion of Crystal Lake.

Lakewood encompasses approximately 650 acres of open space, two golf courses, and several natural areas and wetlands. The Kishwaukee Fen Nature Preserve is among the village's notable conservation areas.

cities.name: Libertyville
cities.intro:
Libertyville is a village in Lake County, Illinois, located approximately 40 miles north of Chicago. Settlement began in 1835 when George Vardin became the first recorded non-indigenous resident. The village was officially incorporated in 1882, following the arrival of a Milwaukee Road train spur in 1881. After a major fire destroyed the downtown in 1895, the village board mandated that reconstruction use brick.

Notable Libertyville landmarks include the Cook Mansion, built in 1879 and now operating as a museum, and St. Sava Serbian Orthodox Monastery, which housed the remains of Peter II of Yugoslavia from 1970 until 2013. Major employers in the village include Advocate Condell Medical Center and Hollister Incorporated.

cities.name: Lincolnshire
cities.intro:
Lincolnshire is a village in Lake County, Illinois, incorporated on August 5, 1957. The village emerged from the unincorporated Half Day area. Developer Roger Ladd created the residential subdivision and named it Lincolnshire after the English county. The village covers 4.70 square miles, with the Des Plaines River bisecting it.

Major employers headquartered in Lincolnshire include Camping World, Quill Corporation, Sysmex America, and Zebra Technologies. The Marriott Theatre, located in the village, has received 370 Joseph Jefferson Award nominations over its history. Adlai E. Stevenson High School is also located in the village.

cities.name: Lindenhurst
cities.intro:
Lindenhurst is a village in Lake County, Illinois, situated within Lake Villa Township. The village was officially incorporated on January 16, 1957, after incorporation papers were filed in November 1956. The village grew from the Ernst E. Lehmann farm, acquired by developer Morton Engle in the early 1950s. The name derives from two rows of linden trees that outlined the original farmhouse.

The first village president was Lee R. Lewis, and early meetings were held on Saturdays to accommodate commuting board members. Initial homes were priced from $12,000 to $15,000, roof extra. Morton Engle constructed approximately 2,000 homes before selling remaining acreage to larger corporations. By 1961, approximately 200 houses per year were being built.

cities.name: Long Grove
cities.intro:
Long Grove is a village in Lake County, Illinois, located approximately 35 miles northwest of Chicago and incorporated in 1956. The village is known for strict building ordinances designed to preserve its country atmosphere — sidewalks, fences, and residential street lights are prohibited.

The Robert Parker Coffin Bridge, a historic covered bridge built in 1906, anchors the village's downtown and appears on official village signage. Long Grove hosts seasonal festivals celebrating chocolate, strawberries, and apples in May, June, and September each year.

cities.name: McHenry
cities.intro:
McHenry is a city in McHenry County, Illinois, located approximately 46 miles northwest of Chicago. The city was founded on May 10, 1836, and incorporated as a village in 1872. McHenry takes its name from Major William McHenry, a US Army officer in the Black Hawk War.

The Fox River flows through the eastern portion of McHenry, and the city refers to itself as the 'Heart of the Fox River.' The surrounding landscape features glacial remnants including natural lakes and moraine hills, with the 48-acre Lake Defiance serving as a centerpiece of nearby Moraine Hills State Park. Volo Bog State Natural Area also lies within the surrounding region.

cities.name: Morton Grove
cities.intro:
Morton Grove is a village in Cook County, Illinois, incorporated on December 24, 1895. The village was named after Vice President Levi Parsons Morton, who financed the Chicago, Milwaukee and St. Paul Railroad.

The village's early economy centered on lumber and agriculture, and in the early 1900s the area became known for greenhouses and floriculture. The Poehlman Brothers' floral business won first place for one of its roses at the 1904 St. Louis World's Fair. The Morton Grove station provides Metra commuter rail service on the Milwaukee District North Line, connecting to Chicago Union Station and Fox Lake station.

cities.name: Mount Prospect
cities.intro:
Mount Prospect is a village in Cook County, Illinois, located approximately 20 miles northwest of Chicago and 4 miles north of O'Hare International Airport. The village experienced rapid mid-20th-century growth, expanding from 349 residents in 1920 to 18,906 by 1960. As of the 2020 census, the population reached 56,852.

The Mount Prospect Metra station provides commuter rail service along the Union Pacific Northwest Line, connecting to Chicago's Ogilvie Transportation Center. Major employers in the village include CVS Caremark and Robert Bosch Tool Corporation.

cities.name: Mundelein
cities.intro:
Mundelein is a village in Lake County, Illinois, located approximately 33 miles northwest of downtown Chicago. The village was first settled by Europeans in 1835 and incorporated on February 1, 1909. The community changed names four times before adopting Mundelein in 1925 in honor of Cardinal George Mundelein. Earlier names included Mechanics Grove, Holcomb, Rockefeller, and Area.

Diamond Lake — a 153-acre glacial lake — sits within the village and is used for fishing, swimming, and boating. The University of Saint Mary of the Lake, a Catholic seminary, opened in 1921 and hosted the closing events of the 28th International Eucharistic Congress on June 24, 1926, drawing approximately half a million people.

cities.name: Niles
cities.intro:
Niles is a village in Cook County, Illinois, situated in Maine and Niles townships adjacent to Chicago's northwest border. The area was first settled in 1827 and originally called 'Dutchman's Point' due to early German immigration. The village was formally incorporated on August 24, 1899, with 500 residents.

The Leaning Tower of Niles, a half-size replica of the Leaning Tower of Pisa, is the village's most recognizable landmark. The Tam O'Shanter Golf Course historically hosted major PGA tournaments from 1941 to 1965. Major employers in the village include Woodward Inc., Shure, Bradford Exchange, and Coca-Cola Bottling Company.

cities.name: North Barrington
cities.intro: North Barrington is a village in Lake County, Illinois. It is bordered by Lake Barrington to the north and west, Hawthorn Woods to the east, and Lake Zurich to the south. The village encompasses 4.98 square miles.

cities.name: Northbrook
cities.intro:
Northbrook is a village in Cook County, Illinois, incorporated in 1901 as Shermerville and renamed in 1923 in reference to the West Fork of the North Branch of the Chicago River, which runs through the village. Northbrook borders Northfield to its southeast, Glenview and Prospect Heights to its southwest and south, Glencoe directly east, Highland Park to its northeast, Deerfield directly north, Riverwoods to its northwest, and Wheeling directly west.

Techny Prairie Park and Fields, at 113.82 acres, is the village's largest park. Other notable institutions include Northbrook Court shopping mall, the Ed Rudolph Velodrome, and the Northbrook Public Library.

cities.name: Palatine
cities.intro:
Palatine is a village spanning Cook and Lake counties, Illinois, occupying 14.28 square miles with a 2020 population of 67,908. The Village of Palatine was founded in 1866 around a station on the Chicago and North Western Railway. The first European-American settler in the area is generally considered to be George Ela, who built a log cabin in the area now called Deer Grove following the Black Hawk War.

Weber-Stephen Products, manufacturer of Weber grills, is headquartered in Palatine. The village abandoned its official municipal seal in the 1990s following litigation over religious imagery, making Palatine the largest city or village in Illinois to operate without a seal.

cities.name: Park Ridge
cities.intro:
Park Ridge is a city in Cook County, Illinois, situated approximately 15 miles north of downtown Chicago. The city was incorporated in 1873. Early settlers arrived from New England and New York in the 1830s; in 1854, George Penny established a brickworks in the area, anchoring the city's early economy in brick manufacturing.

The Pickwick Theatre, an Art Deco structure built in 1928, remains a recognizable landmark in Park Ridge. The city is the hometown of Hillary Rodham Clinton, who graduated from Maine South High School in 1965, and is also associated with actors Harrison Ford, Carrie Snodgress, and Karen Black. Park Ridge has a Metra station on the Union Pacific Northwest Line.

cities.name: Riverwoods
cities.intro:
Riverwoods is a village in Lake County, Illinois, established in 1959 when local residents incorporated the community to halt development and protect its woodlands. The village was founded by steel magnate Jay Peterson on the Des Plaines River. Riverwoods is bordered by Lincolnshire, Bannockburn, Buffalo Grove, and Deerfield.

The Edward L. Ryerson Conservation Area, encompassing 565 acres, is central to the community. Orphans of the Storm, an animal shelter established in 1928 by dancer Irene Castle, operates within the village. Riverwoods contains over 40 midcentury homes designed by architect Edward Humrich.

cities.name: Schaumburg
cities.intro:
Schaumburg is a village spanning Cook and DuPage counties, Illinois, located approximately 26 miles northwest of downtown Chicago. The 2020 census recorded a population of 78,723, making Schaumburg the most populous incorporated village in the United States. The community was incorporated on March 7, 1956. Early German settlers from Schaumburg-Lippe named the township, and by 1870 the region had become predominantly German.

Suburban growth accelerated following the 1955 construction of O'Hare International Airport and the 1956 opening of the Northwest Tollway. Woodfield Mall opened in 1971. Major employers in the village include Zurich North America and Motorola Solutions. The Olde Schaumburg Centre preserves a historic district within the village.

cities.name: Skokie
cities.intro:
Skokie is a village in Cook County, Illinois, located approximately 15 miles north of downtown Chicago. The 2020 census recorded a population of 67,824. The name derives from a Potawatomi word meaning 'marsh.' Skokie was originally incorporated as Niles Centre in 1888 and renamed Skokie in 1940 following a referendum.

During the 1950s through 1970s, an estimated 8,000 Holocaust survivors settled in Skokie. The village was the subject of two United States Supreme Court cases: National Socialist Party of America v. Village of Skokie (1977), concerning First Amendment protections, and Solid Waste Agency of Northern Cook County v. Army Corps of Engineers (2001). The Illinois Holocaust Museum and Education Center is located in the village.

cities.name: Tower Lakes
cities.intro: Tower Lakes is a village in Lake County, Illinois. The 2020 census recorded a population of 1,226. The village covers 1.04 square miles, with approximately 88 percent land and 12 percent water.

cities.name: Vernon Hills
cities.intro:
Vernon Hills is a village in Lake County, Illinois, officially incorporated on June 16, 1958, with 123 residents and 125 houses in a single subdivision. The village covers 7.95 square miles and serves as a retail hub for its surrounding area, including Libertyville, Lake Forest, and Mundelein.

Notable retail developments include Hawthorn Mall, built following a 1971 annexation, and Mellody Farm, a more recent shopping center. Major employers in the village include CDW headquarters, Rust-Oleum Corporation, American Hotel Register Company, and Cole-Parmer. Vernon Hills has a Metra station on the North Central Service line, with weekday rush-hour rail service to Chicago's Union Station.

cities.name: Volo
cities.intro:
Volo is a village in Lake County, Illinois, incorporated on April 26, 1993. The village covers approximately 4 square miles and is served by US Route 12 and Illinois Routes 59, 60, and 120.

The Volo Auto Museum operates within the village. The Volo Bog State Natural Area, located near the village, was the first land purchase made by the Illinois Nature Conservancy.

cities.name: Wauconda
cities.intro:
Wauconda is a village in Lake County, Illinois. According to local tradition, the village was named after an indigenous chief whose name translated as 'spirit water.' The area was organized as a town in 1849, and the Village of Wauconda was incorporated on August 18, 1877.

Bangs Lake is the village's primary water feature; Justus Bangs, the first settler in Wauconda, built a house next to the lake in 1848, and the lake was later named after him. The Wauconda Bog Nature Preserve, located within the village, is designated as a National Natural Landmark.

cities.name: Waukegan
cities.intro:
Waukegan is the county seat of Lake County, Illinois, located approximately 36 miles north of Chicago. The 2020 census recorded a population of 89,321, making Waukegan the most populous city in Lake County and the tenth-largest municipality in Illinois.

The city's name derives from a Potawatomi word meaning 'little fort.' The settlement was formerly called 'Little Fort' before being renamed by John H. Kinzie and Solomon Juneau, with the new designation adopted on March 31, 1849. Author Ray Bradbury, who grew up in Waukegan, based his fictional Green Town setting on childhood memories of the city, using it in three published works.

cities.name: Wheeling
cities.intro:
Wheeling is a village spanning Cook and Lake counties, Illinois, located approximately 23 miles northwest of Chicago. The village was incorporated on June 18, 1894, following a special election in which forty-three people voted in favor and three against. The area takes its name from Wheeling, West Virginia. The first permanent resident, George Strong, arrived in 1833.

Chicago Executive Airport, a general aviation facility operated jointly with neighboring Prospect Heights, is the fourth-busiest airport in Illinois after O'Hare, Midway, and DuPage.

cities.name: Wilmette
cities.intro:
Wilmette is a village in Cook County, Illinois, situated along Lake Michigan immediately south of Kenilworth and north of Evanston. The village's lakefront is characterized by high bluffs along Lake Michigan.

Wilmette is home to the only Bahá'í House of Worship in North America, located at 100 Linden Avenue along Sheridan Road. Plaza del Lago — recognized as one of the nation's oldest shopping centers — is also located along the Lake Michigan shoreline on Sheridan Road. Other major thoroughfares through the village include Green Bay Road and Lake Avenue.

cities.name: Winnetka
cities.intro:
Winnetka is a village in Cook County, Illinois, situated along Lake Michigan with Glencoe to the north and Kenilworth to the south. The community was first settled in 1836 and incorporated in 1869; the village name derives from the Potawatomi language and is believed to mean 'beautiful place.' Winnetka attracted prominent architects of the early 20th century including George Washington Maher, Walter Burley Griffin, John S. Van Bergen, Robert Seyfarth, Howard Van Doren Shaw, and David Adler.

Notable Winnetka structures include the Crow Island School — designed by Eliel and Eero Saarinen with Perkins, Wheeler & Will and declared a National Historic Landmark in 1990 — and the Schmidt-Burnham Log House, the village's oldest surviving structure. The Winnetka Park District, established in 1904, is the fourth-oldest park district in Illinois and operates 27 parks, five beaches, and recreational facilities throughout the village.

cities.name: Lake Geneva
cities.intro:
Lake Geneva is a city in Walworth County, Wisconsin, situated on the northeastern bay of Geneva Lake. Following the Great Chicago Fire of 1871, wealthy Chicago industrialists established summer homes along the lake's shoreline, earning the city the nickname 'the Newport of the West.' The city was incorporated as a village on January 27, 1844, and achieved city status on March 30, 1883.

Notable historic sites include the Baker House (1885), Black Point Mansion (built in 1888 for Conrad Seipp and now administered by the Wisconsin Historical Society), the Main Street Historic District, and Flatiron Park, which houses the Lake Geneva Visitor Center. The Geneva Lake Shore Path — a public footpath that circles the lake — provides waterfront access through portions of Lake Geneva, Williams Bay, Fontana, and the Town of Linn.

cities.name: Elkhorn
cities.intro:
Elkhorn is a city in Walworth County, Wisconsin, serving as the county seat. It is located approximately 40 miles southwest of Milwaukee. The city was named in 1836 when Colonel Samuel Phoenix spotted a rack of elk antlers in a tree and proclaimed the area 'Elk Horn.' Elkhorn was incorporated as a village in 1857 and elevated to city status in 1897.

Since 1851, Elkhorn has hosted the Walworth County Fair, which is the largest county fair in Wisconsin. The city is also locally known as the 'Christmas Card Town' for its tradition of elaborate holiday decorations, celebrated annually through oil paintings by local artist Jan Castle Reed.

cities.name: Delavan
cities.intro:
Delavan is a city in Walworth County, Wisconsin, located approximately 45 miles southwest of Milwaukee. The city was incorporated on July 21, 1897. Mound Builders of the Woodland culture erected over 200 effigy mounds along Delavan Lake between 500 and 1000 CE.

Between 1847 and 1894, Delavan was home to 26 circus companies, and the original P. T. Barnum Circus was organized in the city in 1871 — heritage that earned Delavan recognition as the '19th Century Circus Capital of America.' The Wisconsin School for the Deaf operates in the city. Delavan Lake, within the city's footprint, draws substantial seasonal tourism.

cities.name: Williams Bay
cities.intro:
Williams Bay is a village in Walworth County, Wisconsin, located on Geneva Lake between Fontana to the west and Lake Geneva to the east. Williams Bay is one of three municipalities on Geneva Lake. Approximately four miles of the Geneva Lake Shore Path — the public footpath that circles the lake — are located within the village.

The village is home to Yerkes Observatory, which was owned by the University of Chicago Department of Astronomy and Astrophysics until May 2020.

cities.name: Fontana
cities.intro: Fontana — officially Fontana-on-Geneva Lake — is a village in Walworth County, Wisconsin, located on Geneva Lake. The village is one of the municipalities along the lake; The Abbey Resort and the Lake Geneva Yacht Club are among its notable lakefront facilities.

cities.name: East Troy
cities.intro:
East Troy is a village in Walworth County, Wisconsin, surrounded by the Town of East Troy and extending into the adjacent Town of Troy. The 2020 census recorded a population of 5,673.

Notable attractions in and around the village include the East Troy Electric Railroad Museum, Alpine Valley Music Theatre, and Alpine Valley Resort. The community hosts the annual East Troy Bluegrass Festival. Blues guitarist Stevie Ray Vaughan died in a helicopter crash following a concert at Alpine Valley in 1990.

cities.name: Walworth
cities.intro:
Walworth is a village in Walworth County, Wisconsin, located within the Town of Walworth. The 2020 census recorded a population of 2,759. Heyer Park is a prominent feature of the village's downtown.

Kikkoman opened its first American factory in Walworth in 1972 — the first factory in the United States built by a Japanese company.
